steps.prompt: How high does the ladder reach?

graphic.name: ladder
graphic.caption: A ladder leans against a wall. The wall, the ground, and the ladder make a right triangle — and the three sides are linked by one exact rule.

steps.body:
A right triangle has one square corner (a 90° angle). The two sides that meet there are the legs, [a](blue) and [b](orange).
The side across from the square corner — always the longest — is the [hypotenuse c](green).
This whole lesson is one rule that links the two legs to the hypotenuse.

steps.prompt: Drag a corner and watch the sides
steps.subtitle: The longest side always sits opposite the biggest angle. When one corner is a square corner (90°), that longest side is the hypotenuse. Drag and see the lengths change together.
A skate ramp rises [6 m](blue) straight up over an [8 m](orange) run along the ground. Drag to estimate the length of the [sloping ramp](green) — that's the hypotenuse.

feedback.incorrect: The slope is longer than each leg but shorter than 6 + 8 = 14. Square the legs, add, then take the square root.

steps.revealText: It's 10 m. 6² + 8² = 36 + 64 = 100, and √100 = 10. Notice the hypotenuse is longer than either leg, but less than 6 + 8.

steps.body:
In any right triangle, the squares of the two legs add up to the square of the [hypotenuse](green):
[a](blue)² + [b](orange)² = [c](green)².
Think of a real square built on each side — the two smaller squares exactly fill the big one.
For legs [3](blue) and [4](orange): 3² + 4² = 9 + 16 = 25, so [c](green)² = 25. To get [c](green) itself, take the square root: √25 = 5.

stages: Take four copies of our right triangle, each with legs a and b and hypotenuse c.
Pack all four into a square of side a + b. The tilted gap left in the middle is a square on the hypotenuse, so its area is c².
Now slide the same four triangles into the corners a different way. The leftover space is two squares — one a by a, the other b by b.
Same four triangles, same big square, so the two leftover areas must be equal: a² + b² = c².

steps.prompt: Which triples make a right triangle?
steps.subtitle: Square the two shorter sides and add them. If the total equals the longest side squared, the triple is a right triangle. Tap a card, then tap its group.
steps.doneNote: A triple is a right triangle exactly when a² + b² = c². 3-4-5, 6-8-10, and 5-12-13 all fit; 2-3-4 (4 + 9 ≠ 16) and 4-5-6 (16 + 25 ≠ 36) fall short.

steps.subtitle: A student finds the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs 6 and 8. One line is wrong — tap it.

lines.text: The legs are a = 6 and b = 8.

steps.explanation: You have to square the legs first: 6^2 + 8^2 = 36 + 64 = 100. Then take the square root: c = sqrt(100) = 10, not 14. Adding 6 + 8 skips both the squaring and the square root.
steps.hint: Line 2 squares the legs, but the next line just adds them. Square first, add, then take the square root.
steps.doneNote: Exactly — square the legs before adding, and don't forget the square root.

problems.prompt: A right triangle has legs of 3 and 4. Find the hypotenuse.

feedback.incorrect: In a right triangle, leg² + leg² = hypotenuse².

problems.strongHint: The hypotenuse c has c² = 3² + 4² = 25. Take the square root.

problems.prompt: A right triangle has a leg of 5 and a hypotenuse of 13. Find the other leg.

feedback.incorrect: Rearrange a² + b² = c²: the missing leg² = hypotenuse² − known leg².

problems.strongHint: The missing leg b has b² = 13² − 5² = 144. Take the square root.

problems.prompt: A right triangle has legs of 8 and 15. Find the hypotenuse.

feedback.incorrect: In a right triangle, leg² + leg² = hypotenuse².

problems.strongHint: The hypotenuse c has c² = 8² + 15² = 289. Take the square root.

steps.subtitle: A right triangle has a leg of 9 and a hypotenuse of 15. Find the other leg.

feedback.incorrect: The missing leg² = hypotenuse² − known leg² = 15² − 9².
